lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: I pray thee, good Mercutio, let’s retire:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: The day is hot, the Capulets abroad,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And, if we meet, we shall not scape a brawl;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: For now, these hot days, is the mad blood stirring.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Thou art like one of those fellows that when he enters the

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: confines of a tavern claps me his sword upon the table and

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: says “God send me no need of thee!” and by the operation

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: of the second cup draws it on the drawer, when indeed there

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: is no need.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Am I like such a fellow?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Come, come, thou art as hot a Jack in thy mood as

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: any in Italy, and as soon moved to be moody, and as

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: soon moody to be moved.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And what to?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Nay, an there were two such, we should have none shortly, for

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: one would kill the other. Thou! why, thou wilt quarrel with a

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: man that hath a hair more, or a hair less, in his

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: beard, than thou hast: thou wilt quarrel with a man for cracking

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: nuts, having no other reason but because thou hast hazel eyes: what

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: eye but such an eye would spy out such a quarrel? Thy

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: head is as full of quarrels as an egg is full of

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: meat, and yet thy head hath been beaten as addle as an

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: egg for quarrelling: thou hast quarrelled with a man for coughing in

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: the street, because he hath wakened thy dog that hath lain asleep

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: in the sun: didst thou not fall out with a tailor for

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: wearing his new doublet before Easter? with another, for tying his new

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: shoes with old riband? and yet thou wilt tutor me from quarrelling!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: An I were so apt to quarrel as thou art, any man

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: should buy the fee-simple of my life for an hour and a

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: quarter.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: The fee-simple! O simple!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: By my head, here come the Capulets.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: By my heel, I care not.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Follow me close, for I will speak to them. Gentlemen, good den:

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: a word with one of you.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And but one word with one of us? couple it with something;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: make it a word and a blow.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: You shall find me apt enough to that, sir, an you will give me occasion.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Could you not take some occasion without giving?

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Mercutio, thou consort’st with Romeo—

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Consort! what, dost thou make us minstrels? an thou make minstrels of

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: us, look to hear nothing but discords: here’s my fiddlestick; here’s that

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: shall make you dance. ’Zounds, consort!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: We talk here in the public haunt of men:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Either withdraw unto some private place,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And reason coldly of your grievances,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Or else depart; here all eyes gaze on us.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Men’s eyes were made to look, and let them gaze;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: I will not budge for no man’s pleasure, I.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Well, peace be with you, sir: here comes my man.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: But I’ll be hang’d, sir, if he wear your livery:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Marry, go before to field, he’ll be your follower;

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Your worship in that sense may call him “man.”

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Romeo, the hate I bear thee can afford

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: No better term than this—thou art a villain.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Tybalt, the reason that I have to love thee

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Doth much excuse the appertaining rage

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To such a greeting: villain am I none;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Therefore farewell; I see thou know’st me not.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Boy, this shall not excuse the injuries

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: That thou hast done me; therefore turn and draw.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I do protest, I never injured thee,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But love thee better than thou canst devise,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Till thou shalt know the reason of my love:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And so, good Capulet—which name I tender

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As dearly as my own—be satisfied.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: O calm, dishonourable, vile submission!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Alla stoccata carries it away.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Tybalt, you rat-catcher, will you walk?

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: What wouldst thou have with me?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Good king of cats, nothing but one of your nine lives; that

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: I mean to make bold withal, and, as you shall use me

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: hereafter, dry-beat the rest of the eight. Will you pluck your sword

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: out of his pilcher by the ears? make haste, lest mine be

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: about your ears ere it be out.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: I am for you.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Gentle Mercutio, put thy rapier up.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Come, sir, your passado. They fight.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Draw, Benvolio; beat down their weapons.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Gentlemen, for shame, forbear this outrage!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Tybalt, Mercutio, the prince expressly hath

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Forbidden bandying in Verona streets:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hold, Tybalt! good Mercutio! Tybalt under Romeo’s arm stabs Mercutio, and flies with his followers.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: I am hurt.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: A plague o’ both your houses! I am sped.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Is he gone, and hath nothing?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: What, art thou hurt?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Ay, ay, a scratch, a scratch; marry, ’tis enough.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Where is my page? Go, villain, fetch a surgeon.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Courage, man; the hurt cannot be much.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: No, ’tis not so deep as a well, nor so wide as

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: a church-door; but ’tis enough, ’twill serve: ask for me to-morrow, and

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: you shall find me a grave man. I am peppered, I warrant,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: for this world. A plague o’ both your houses! ’Zounds, a dog,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: a rat, a mouse, a cat, to scratch a man to death!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: a braggart, a rogue, a villain, that fights by the book of

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: arithmetic! Why the devil came you between us? I was hurt under

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: your arm.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I thought all for the best.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Help me into some house, Benvolio,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Or I shall faint. A plague o’ both your houses!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: They have made worms’ meat of me: I have it,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: And soundly too: your houses!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This gentleman, the prince’s near ally,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My very friend, hath got his mortal hurt

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In my behalf; my reputation stain’d

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With Tybalt’s slander—Tybalt, that an hour

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hath been my kinsman! O sweet Juliet,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thy beauty hath made me effeminate

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And in my temper soften’d valour’s steel!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: O Romeo, Romeo, brave Mercutio’s dead!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: That gallant spirit hath aspired the clouds,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Which too untimely here did scorn the earth.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This day’s black fate on more days doth depend;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This but begins the woe others must end.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Here comes the furious Tybalt back again.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Alive, in triumph! and Mercutio slain!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Away to heaven, respective lenity,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And fire-eyed fury be my conduct now!

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Thou, wretched boy, that didst consort him here,

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Shalt with him hence.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This shall determine that. They fight; Tybalt falls.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Romeo, away, be gone!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: The citizens are up, and Tybalt slain.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Stand not amazed: the prince will doom thee death,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: If thou art taken: hence, be gone, away!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, I am fortune’s fool!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Why dost thou stay?

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Which way ran he that kill’d Mercutio?

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Tybalt, that murderer, which way ran he?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: There lies that Tybalt.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Up, sir, go with me;

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: I charge thee in the princes name, obey.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Where are the vile beginners of this fray?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: O noble prince, I can discover all

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: The unlucky manage of this fatal brawl:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: There lies the man, slain by young Romeo,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: That slew thy kinsman, brave Mercutio.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Tybalt, my cousin! O my brother’s child!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: O prince! O cousin! husband! O, the blood is spilt

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Of my dear kinsman! Prince, as thou art true,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: For blood of ours, shed blood of Montague.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: O cousin, cousin!

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Benvolio, who began this bloody fray?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Tybalt, here slain, whom Romeo’s hand did slay;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Romeo that spoke him fair, bade him bethink

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: How nice the quarrel was, and urged withal

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Your high displeasure: all this uttered

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: With gentle breath, calm look, knees humbly bow’d,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Could not take truce with the unruly spleen

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Of Tybalt deaf to peace, but that he tilts

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: With piercing steel at bold Mercutio’s breast,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Who, all as hot, turns deadly point to point,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And, with a martial scorn, with one hand beats

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Cold death aside, and with the other sends

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: It back to Tybalt, whose dexterity

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Retorts it: Romeo he cries aloud,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: “Hold, friends! friends, part!” and, swifter than his tongue,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: His agile arm beats down their fatal points,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And ’twixt them rushes; underneath whose arm

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: An envious thrust from Tybalt hit the life

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Of stout Mercutio, and then Tybalt fled;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: But by and by comes back to Romeo,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Who had but newly entertain’d revenge,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And to’t they go like lightning, for, ere I

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Could draw to part them, was stout Tybalt slain,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And, as he fell, did Romeo turn and fly.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: This is the truth, or let Benvolio die.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: He is a kinsman to the Montague;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Affection makes him false; he speaks not true:

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Some twenty of them fought in this black strife,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: And all those twenty could but kill one life.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: I beg for justice, which thou, prince, must give;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Romeo slew Tybalt, Romeo must not live.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Romeo slew him, he slew Mercutio;

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Who now the price of his dear blood doth owe?

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Not Romeo, prince, he was Mercutio’s friend;

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: His fault concludes but what the law should end,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: The life of Tybalt.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And for that offence

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Immediately we do exile him hence:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I have an interest in your hate’s proceeding,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: My blood for your rude brawls doth lie a-bleeding;

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: But I’ll amerce you with so strong a fine

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: That you shall all repent the loss of mine:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: I will be deaf to pleading and excuses;

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Nor tears nor prayers shall purchase out abuses:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Therefore use none: let Romeo hence in haste,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Else, when he’s found, that hour is his last.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Bear hence this body and attend our will:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Mercy but murders, pardoning those that kill.

stage_directions.text: Enter Mercutio, Benvolio, Page, and Servants.

Now, Tybalt, take the villain back again, That late thou gavest me; for Mercutio’s soul Is but a little way above our heads, Staying for thine to keep him company: Either thou, or I, or both, must go with him.
Enter Prince, attended; Montague, Capulet, their Wives, and others.

annotations.title: 'A plague o' both your houses!' — the play's tragic hinge
annotations.body: Everything before this moment is comedy — arranged or conditioned or delayed. Everything after is tragedy. Mercutio's death converts the register of the whole play. The specific mechanism is cruel: Romeo's well-meaning intervention (stepping between Tybalt and Mercutio to urge peace, citing the Prince's decree) is exactly what enables Tybalt to land the fatal thrust, reaching under Romeo's arm. Romeo's peacemaking causes the death that will force the tragedy. Mercutio delivers three versions of his dying curse, 'A plague o' both your houses,' each more desperate than the last — refusing to side with either family, laying the tragedy's fault equally on both. The curse is structurally performative: from Mercutio's death forward, the play accelerates, every scene shorter and tighter than the last, rushing toward 5.3.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
Susan Snyder, The Comic Matrix of Shakespeare's Tragedies (1979)

annotations.title: Mercutio dying — puns to the last
annotations.body: Mercutio's dying lines keep his voice intact: 'Ask for me to-morrow, and you shall find me a grave man' — the pun on 'grave' (serious / sepulchral) is delivered with a wound in his chest. 'I am peppered, I warrant, for this world' — 'peppered' meaning 'done for.' He keeps fencing with language until his language gives out. This is not just characterization; it is structural. The play cannot continue in Mercutio's voice, and he knows it: 'A plague o' both your houses, / They have made worms' meat of me.' The 'worms' meat' phrase compresses his identity (wit, speed, excess) into the material fact of a corpse. Mercutio's commitment to puns even as he bleeds out is Shakespeare's most concentrated demonstration of character expressed in style.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: 'O, I am fortune's fool!'
annotations.body: After killing Tybalt, Romeo cries out: 'O, I am fortune's fool!' — the phrase naming the play's second theory of misfortune. The prologue said 'star-cross'd' (fated by the stars); here, Romeo says 'fortune's fool' (toy of the goddess Fortuna). Fortune was the medieval figure of circumstance; her wheel raised and toppled men arbitrarily. Romeo is indicating that he is not just doomed, but humiliated by doom — made a fool. The self-awareness is partial: he is not yet ready to rebel against fortune. That moment comes in 5.1, 'Then I defy you, stars!' — where his suicide becomes the paradoxical assertion of will against the fate the prologue announced.
sources: Douglas Peterson, Time, Tide, and Tempest (1973)
Frederick Kiefer, Fortune and Elizabethan Tragedy (1983)

annotations.title: The Prince's second judgment — exile instead of death
annotations.body: Escalus returns to adjudicate the second major breach of the peace. The decision is consequential: Romeo killed Tybalt, but Tybalt killed Mercutio first. The Prince banishes Romeo rather than executing him, and fines the Montagues and Capulets. Every element of this judgment will drive the rest of the play. Banishment rather than death preserves Romeo — and therefore preserves the need to communicate with Juliet — and therefore requires the Friar's letter, which (Act 5) will fail to reach him. The fine does not end the feud; the families' private grief takes a more personal turn. The Prince's closing line, 'Mercy but murders, pardoning those that kill,' is a rueful acknowledgment that his attempted mercy will not save more lives.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: Why is Mercutio's death called the play's tragic hinge?
options: It is the longest death-scene
It converts the play's register from comedy to tragedy — everything before is conditional, everything after is catastrophe
It happens at the exact midpoint (line count)
The Prince appears for the first time

trials.prompt: How exactly does Tybalt manage to wound Mercutio?
options: Tybalt stabs him from behind
Romeo steps between them to urge peace, and Tybalt thrusts under Romeo's arm — Romeo's peacemaking enables the fatal blow
Mercutio trips on his own sword
Benvolio accidentally pushes Mercutio onto Tybalt's blade

trials.prompt: What pun does the dying Mercutio make?
options: A pun on 'feud' / 'food'
'Ask for me to-morrow, and you shall find me a grave man' — grave meaning both serious and buried
A pun on 'Tybalt' / 'Tybert'
A pun on 'Romeo' / 'rose'

trials.prompt: What does Mercutio's triple-repeated curse 'A plague o' both your houses!' accomplish?
options: It blames only the Capulets
It refuses to take sides — laying the tragedy's fault equally on Montague and Capulet
It asks for divine intervention
It is merely an exclamation

trials.prompt: Romeo cries 'O, I am fortune's fool!' — what does this mean?
options: He is proud of his luck
He feels used and humiliated by Fortune (circumstance) — a toy of events beyond his control
He is apologizing to Tybalt
He is joking

trials.prompt: What is the Prince's judgment at the end of the scene?
options: He executes Romeo
He exiles Romeo from Verona on pain of death — and fines both houses; this preserves Romeo for the rest of the tragedy
He pardons Romeo entirely
He jails Romeo for one year
